Revisit the IPSAS and other COA issuances, particularly on the preparation and presentation of restated Financial Statements.

Recommendations
We recommended Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to identify causes of the decreasing collection of local revenues and/or intensify collection efforts by employing various strategies. Also, the Sangguniang Bayan may enact additional sources of income and/or push for the passage of an updated revenue code to boost local taxation and design a systematic and realistic forecasting of revenue collection based on the current state of the Municipality’s economic enterprises.

Continuous customer support be easily available whenever issues are presented.

We recommended and Management agree to include in their CapDev agenda the attendance to seminars on GMEF and Gender Responsive LGU (GeRL) Assessment Tool to capacitate themselves in the conduct of gender audit.

We recommended and Management agreed that the  GFPS will conduct continuous monitoring of the status of implementation of GAD PAPs identified in the GAD Plan and Budget.

Recommendations
We recommended that Management provide adequate and available resources for the collection and processing of data requirements of the GAD Database.
Also, we recommended that Management instruct the GFPS to:
Create and institutionalize the GAD Database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data to provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation to effectively plan and implement GAD-related PAPs;
Coordinate with the various agencies, departments and barangays in updating of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation;
Establish linkage of the existing Community Based Monitoring System (CBMS) Accelerated Poverty Profiling with the GAD sex-disaggregated data or GAD database required under PCW MC No. 2014-05 dated 24 November 2014, to catalyze performance-based gender responsive planning on the basis of a reliable information system.
Ensure that the gender issues contained in the ensuing GPB are based from the results of gender analysis conducted on the GAD Database and/or any other gender analysis tools.

We recommended and Management through the Local Chief Executive agreed to continue improving the internal control system of the LGU by addressing the remaining gaps presented.

The LCE will instruct the MSWDO to Coordinate with partner agencies such as funeral parlors, drugstores/pharmacy, hospitals and other government agencies where clients may be referred to be assisted/attended, through a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA).

We recommended and Management throught the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure that the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE as provided in COA Circular 2020-006 are strictly observed to fast track and facilitate the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances by instructing the:
Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) and submit the same to the COA Audit Team at least ten calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities in compliance with the pertinent provisions of Section 5 of the Circular.
After each office of the LGU to filled up the Inventory Count Form it shall be submitted to the Property Unit for verification. The form will then be used by the Inventory Committee as basis in the preparation of its PIP and will be considered as part of their inventory working paper.
Inventory Committee to record/document the physical count daily using the ICF per PPE sub-major account group and will be the basis in the preparation of RPCPPE after the physical count.
Prepared List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs (Annex C of the Circular) be supported with documents under Paragraph 7 and 8 of the Circular and submit these lists to the Accounting Unit for recording in the books of accounts of the LGU to establish the existence and proper accountability of the PPE accounts.
Moreover, we also recommended and Management agreed to expedite the completion of the one-time cleansing to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

Thereafter, the CAO shall take-up the verified balance in the City’s books of accounts to adjust the ending balances of both the inventory and donation accounts, if warranted. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to ensure that the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE as provided in COA Circular 2020-006 are strictly observed to facilitate the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances by instructing the:
Inventory Committee to assign a unique Property Number for each PPE item following the required code format in the numbering system as provided in Section 5 of the Circular.
Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) and submit the same to the COA Audit Team at least ten calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities in compliance with the pertinent provisions of Section 5 of the Circular.
Each office of the LGU to fill up the Inventory Count Form and submit the same to the Property Unit for verification. The form will then be used by the Inventory Committee as basis in the preparation of its PIP and will be considered as part of their inventory working paper.
Inventory Committee to record/document the physical count daily using the ICF per PPE sub-major account group and will be the basis in the preparation of RPCPPE after the physical count.
Property Unit to  prepare a List of PPEs Found at Station (Annex B of the Circular) and List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs (Annex C of the Circular) during physical inventory and submit these lists to the Accounting Unit for recording in the books of accounts of the LGU to establish the existence and proper accountability of the PPE accounts.

In addition, the LGU shall coordinate with the barangay officials in the observance of the prompt claim of said share from Real Property Tax (RPT) and Community Tax Certificate (CTC) and/or other claims.

The Municipal Treasurer to observe the guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 particularly the transfer of unspent allotment of Current Year LDRMM Fund to its bank account at year-end; and
The Municipal Accountant to carefully monitor the balances per ledgers, schedules and reports and reconcile the balance of the Trust Liability-DRRM with the Cash-in Bank 5% Calamity fund once the actual cash was transferred by the MTO to the LDRRM bank account in order to establish a sufficient cash back-up for the said trust account. Also, follow-up from the Sanguniang Panlalawigan the budget review and not rely on the 90-day period for the automatic approval of the Annual Budget.

The Accounting Office to stop the practice of processing transactions that have incomplete documents.

Municipal Accountant derecognized the donated lot in the PPE Account representing transfer of ownership.  A Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) should be prepared by the accounting unit and furnished a copy to COA for reference.

Consider assigning personnel/s to focus on this area to ensure settlement/compliance of this long outstanding issue.

Prevent or detect loss of assets and records on a timely basis; and

Provide reasonable assurance that those procedures and rules are adhered to by the agency personnel.

We recommended that management:
Initiate the needed coordination, monitoring and evaluation on the implementation to assure that all unimplemented projects and programs be given immediate action and implementation. Such that, the execution of the respective responsibilities of the Local Development Council, Executive Committee and of the Local Chief Executive as mandated must be strictly adhered.
Faithfully adhere to the policies and procedures in coming up with a supplemental budget or for any changes in the approved Annual Budget, especially for special funds like the 20% DF, by proper evaluation of the status of PPAs, which will be the basis in reverting programs and projects or to establish savings therefrom as source of appropriation; and
Onwards, require the LGU Planning and Development Office to submit a yearly cumulative status of the PPAs, specifically under the 20% DF to facilitate monitoring and evaluation there.
Management Comment
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct the municipal assessor to continue to communicate with the OPA to initiate the province wide assessment and to plan and implement the expected update of the Assessment Rolls as to the conditions of the real properties, its actual use, and the exemptions on RPT, if any, and to regularly conduct general revision of assessment of real property every three (3) years in accordance with the existing laws, rules and regulations.
Management Comment
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Economic Enterprise Officer to prepare the 5-Year Staffing Plan and Staff Transition Strategy and Five (5) Year Business Plan for each Local Economic Enterprise. Also, prepare the semi-annual SAFSSR needed to support the FFSE.
Further, to submit a copy of the FFSE and FS as basis for evaluation for the decision made.
Management Comment
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
assign a personnel or team who shall take charge of the proper identification and documentation of properties recorded in the books of CGN;
require the inventory committee to timely perform their duties or functions in conducting actual physical count of PPEs for a specified period of time, to facilitate proper accounting and reporting and correct classification of fixed properties; and
direct the reconciliation of records between the GSO and the Accounting Office to present accurate property records.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Mayor:
strictly require the City Accountant to prepare bank reconciliation statements in the manner prescribed by existing regulations for all funds (current and time deposit accounts);
exercise LGU’s authority granted under Section 74 and 111 of P.D. 1445 until they are able to prepare and submit bank reconciliation statements for all funds to the COA Audit Office concerned; and
require the CTO and CAO to reconcile and properly communicate to each other any discrepancies or changes made in the balances reported.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to:
facilitate the creation of an effective procedure in monitoring and enforcing the submission of liquidation reports, progress reports and terminal reports of the projects in coordination with concerned departments of the City;
require the City Accountant to record receipt of fund transfers only upon ensuring that source documents, particularly, the Memorandum of Agreement is attached to the Journal Voucher as required under COA Circular 2021-001; and
direct the City Treasurer to return the unutilized funds to the source NGAs concerned or otherwise request for appropriate authority to use said funds, if deemed appropriate.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
direct the City Accounting Office and Barangay Accounting Section, in coordination with Barangay Officials to identify the fund transfers and projects that remain outstanding as of year-end and immediately liquidate the same;
direct the City Accounting Office to closely monitor the fund transfers and complete the details in the subsidiary ledgers to facilitate the reconciliation with the Barangay Accounting Section; and
instruct the Barangay Accounting Section to update their records to facilitate the reconciliation and identification of the status of the projects with the City Accounting Office.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
require the City Accountant and City Treasurer to coordinate and communicate with each other the necessary information that need to be disclosed on the Notes to Financial Statements;
require the City Accountant to revise the submitted Notes to Financial Statements to provide a fair presentation and reliable information to the users of the financial statements on the transactions affecting Cash Local Treasury account; and
to prudently comply with the standards on the Presentation of the Financial Statements as well as rules on depositing in full and intact, daily or not later than next business day, all collections made by CGN.

We recommended that Management:
strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-386 specifically on the disposal of supplies or properties;
direct the General Service Officer to provide copy of the IIRUP and WMR to the City Accountant as required in the NGAS Manual to be used as basis in derecognizing the properties in accordance with IPSAS 17, to present fairly the balances in the financial statements; and
City Accountant submit the depreciation schedule of the PPE accounts under the TF book for the Audit Team’s validation/verification on the correctness of the depreciation charges.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to:
Immediately submit the lacking BRS for review and verification, and henceforth, observe the prescribed period in the submission to the Audit Team pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011;
Adopt other measures to get the Bank Statements on time such as enrolling the bank accounts online and by sending a letter request to the servicing banks to comply with the timely preparation of the BRS; and
Designate additional personnel to prepare the bank reconciliation statements.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant and the Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to submit the lacking documents and henceforth, ensure that all disbursements of public funds are supported with complete documentation as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC Secretariat to submit the proof of the conduct of pre-procurement conferences such as Minutes of the Meeting and the Attendance Sheets on the above-mentioned 11 projects and henceforth, comply with the provisions of Section 20 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and attach proof in the conduct thereof.

We recommended that Management submit a written explanation on the noted deficiency, and henceforth, strictly observe the provisions of RA No. 9184 especially in adopting the alternative mode of procurement.

We recommended that Management:
Adhere with the provisions of Revenue Regulation Nos. 11-2018 and 13-2018, specifically on the withholding of creditable value added and expanded taxes from all GMP to sellers of goods and services and effect the correct deductions of the corresponding taxes from the amount due to the consultants and remit accordingly to the BIR; and
Instruct the City Treasurer to issue checks for payment of services directly to the consultants except in cases where the needed services are extremely urgent and necessary which could not be conveniently paid through check.

We recommended that Management submit a copy of the authority to purchase the above-mentioned motor vehicles from the Secretary of DBM as required in DILG Memorandum Circular 2021-004 to avoid issuance of Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance.

We recommended that Management direct the City General Services Officer to:
Verify the actual custodian of the items that are still under the accountability of officials and employees who are no longer connected with the City and issue thereafter the necessary AREs to establish proper accountability;
Submit an updated RPCPPE to the Audit Team incorporating the new AREs issued; and
Ensure proper turn-over of property accountabilities of employees resigning or retiring from government service so that property records will be updated on time.

We recommended that Management submit a written explanation on the above noted deficiency and submit the lacking documentary requirements with a reminder that unless duly justified, the same will be issued with Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance.
Henceforth, we recommended that Management strictly observe the policies and procedures set forth under COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advance.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the City Treasurer to issue check for payment of goods and services to the supplier or dealer except in cases where needed items are extremely necessary and urgent which cannot be conveniently paid by check; and
Require the concerned City Officials to comply with the pertinent auditing rules and regulations, particularly the imprest system, and to ensure the withholding of taxes from the payments to contractors and suppliers.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant and concerned officials to submit the above lacking documents and comply with the noted deficiencies.  Henceforth, ensure that all disbursements are supported with complete documentary requirements as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 duly signed/certified/approved by proper officials.

We recommended that Management:
Submit justification/explanation why these cash donations are still not fully utilized for the City’s COVID-19 activities.
Direct the City Treasurer to prepare and submit separate the RCD for DRRM on the cash donations received during the year, and henceforth, maintain the said report for future transactions;
Direct the City Budget Officer to prepare and submit the RCDU for the cash donations received during the year, and henceforth, maintain the said registry as part of their records
Direct the City Accountant to prepare and submit the Report on the Receipt and Utilization of Cash Donations following the format prescribed in COA Circular No. 2014-002 to the OCD copy furnished the Audit Team, and henceforth, observe the timely submission of the same.

We recommended and Management through the City General Services Officer agreed to submit immediately the lacking supporting documents of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances as required, and henceforth, strictly observe the submission thereof within the prescribed period to the Audit Team to facilitate timely review and verification.

We recommended that the Management direct the City Accountant to remit immediately to the BIR the taxes withheld and DST collected in 2021 and in the prior years.  Henceforth, taxes withheld and DST collected shall be remitted not later than the 10th day and 5th day, respectively, of the following month to protect the interest of the government and to avoid the payment of penalties which will be the personal liability of the personnel-in-charge.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to compute the taxes due on the above-mentioned transactions and thereafter demand from the suppliers the amount computed, otherwise, the latter shall be held liable on the noted deficiency.
Henceforth, we recommend that Management adhere with the provisions of RA 8424 or the Tax Code of the Philippines and its related pronouncements issued by the BIR i.e., Revenue Regulations Nos. 11-2018 and 13-2018, specifically in the withholding of creditable value added and expanded taxes from all Government Money Payments to sellers of goods and services and remit accordingly to the BIR.

We recommended that the Finance Management to:
Instruct the Accountable Officers, Collecting Agents and Treasurer to strictly comply with the preparation and submission of the monthly RAAF as basis for monitoring the movement of accountable forms at their possession to ensure reliability of the monthly balances of inventory; and
Prepare the Consolidated RAAF in the proper form and presentation and submit it accompanied by individual reports at the end of each period.

We recommended that:
The Finance Committee require the concerned Accountable Officers to render accounts and subsequent turnover of accountabilities in accordance with the policies, rules, regulations and procedures and with all other related accounting and auditing rules and regulations. 
The Human Resource Management Division (HRMD)/Administrative Division to ensure that proper turn-over of accountabilities is effected in cases of change/s in assignments of accountable officers. Clearances from LGUs finance and other departments, as well as, clearance issued from COA, are mandatory. Hence, notice duly informing COA is necessary to perform cash examination as a prerequisite in issuance of clearance from accountability.

We recommended that the management faithfully adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 in all of its procurement activities so as not to defeat the purpose of the law to have a judicious use of the scarce government resources; and to submit the required supporting documents, explanations and/or compliance of requirements as indicated in respective voucher to facilitate proper disposition of subject transaction documents and that vouchers onwards be duly processed and approved before they will be paid to avoid possible suspension and/or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that management require the accounting unit personnel:

To observe proper recording of transactions, maintain respective records which contains complete data/information, which in turn will support the accounts as appearing in the financial statements; and 
To prepare the JEV to adjust and/or correct entries, as necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office, as in-charge of the keeping of the books of accounts, use the proper accounts under the IPSAS, and faithfully adhere to its implementation to ensure a fair presentation of Trust Fund accounts in the Municipality’s financial statements; submit a correct and accurate financial report considering the receipt and utilization of TF accounts and the corresponding accounts relative to its receipt, utilization and liquidation.

We recommended that the BAC invite in writing a representative from COA as observer in stages of the procurement process in order to strictly comply with the provisions of Section 13.1 and 13.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the official drivers of the Local Government and other concerned personnel to prepare and submit the Monthly Report of Official Travel and the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977. The Monthly reports will be the summary of trips per vehicle in chronological order of the serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets.

We recommended that all accountable officers to remit and deposit their collection intact at the end of the day as required by applicable laws, rules and regulations.  Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer or Liquidating Officer should always monitor the activity of her staff most particularly those relating to the collection and liquidation aspects as well as in the receipt and issuance of accountable forms to ensure that loss or wastage of government funds and property arising from theft,  malversation and  misuse are prevented.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant should coordinate each other and regularly reconcile the cashbook balances with that appearing per books of account of the LGU monthly or at least quarterly for timely adjustments discrepancies/errors for accurate and fair financial statement presentation.

The CSWDO to verify the status of the above beneficiaries and check whether they were disqualified from receiving the second tranche of the cash assistance. 

Further, if these beneficiaries were still included in the 2nd tranche, we recommended that the CSWDO facilitate recommendation for the return of cash assistance received from LGU.

observations.managementAction:
Management reiterated their response to the AOM dated March 10, 2021, as follows:

To state that the BAC did not require the submission of PCAB Licence from the contractors as part of the eligibility requirements is misleading and does not depict the actual scenario. 

The projects subject to the AOM are procured through alternative method of procurement (AMP) - small value. Scrutiny of eligibility-technical requirements, especially the presence of PCAB license, was made in bid projects. The BAC had justified in its written response and during the exit conference that to address this audit memorandum, it issued guidelines in the issuance of BAC Certificate of Eligibility in the year 2017 for procurement of infrastructure projects made through AMP. The guidelines were reiterated in June 2018 and to ensure that a similar situation is prevented, the BAC resolved that all infrastructure projects shall be procured through competitive bidding. 

All these initiatives were made to address the said audit memorandum to ensure that the procurement law is better implemented by the offices in the local government of the City of Naga, Cebu.

observations.recommendation:
The Management to:

Comply strictly with the provisions of Section 89 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 970002 dated February 10, 1997 and COA Circular 20120001 dated June 14, 2012; and

Require the City Accountant to request for the write-off of cash advances of the deceased persons in accordance with COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016
observations.managementAction:
As to compliance of COA Circular 97-002, the City Government has been compliant in making sure that the provisions of said circular are followed. No cash advance was given in violation of the requisites established in the Circular. The City Government has also been prompt in sending out demand letters as soon as the reglementary period for the liquidation of such cash advances is about to expire. 

With respect to the write-off of cash advance of deceased persons, one of the requirements is to have a copy of the PSA Death Certificate, which is only given through the requests of the family. As of this time, the Accounting Office has been unable to track down family members of the deceased Accountable Officers in order to get their cooperation. The Office will attempt to request write-off based on the remaining available options provided for in Section 8.3 of COA Circular 2016-005; however, these are still subject to the approval of the COA. 

Lastly, as stated in the AOM that it is Accountant who can “cause or order the withholding of the payment of the salary of the employee for failure to liquidate his/her cash advance.” COA Circular 97-002 clearly states on Section 9.3.2 that it is the Auditor who can do such act and not the Accountant. Provision of which is read as follows:

“9.3.2 If thirty (30) days have elapsed after demand letter is served and no liquidation or explanation is received, or the explanation received is not satisfactory, the Auditor shall advise the head of Agency to cause or order the withholding of the payment of any money due the AO.”

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management to desist from adopting the reimbursement basis as mode of procurement of goods or services in contravention of Republic Act No. 9184 and instead effectively implement and conform its procurement activities as laid out in the Annual Procurement Plan. We further recommended management to strictly adhere to the requirement that all payments must be made by check which are directly paid to those suppliers, contractors, dealers and creditor. Likewise, submit written explanation for the non-compliance of the R.A 9184 on the above-mentioned procurements.

Lastly, the Management to withhold the prescribed taxes in all of its procurement of goods and services in accordance with BIR Revenue Regulations.
observations.managementAction:
Management reiterated their response during the Exit Conference with COA:

Reimbursement was used by the City Government as a mode of payment and not as a mode of procurement. The mode of procurement used by the LGU was negotiated procurement- emergency cases as per Section 53.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184. The process of requesting for quotations from supplies (canvassing) under this mode is omitted since it allows the LGU to directly negotiate with a technically, legally and financially capable supplier. The LGU opted for this mode since the major criteria for emergency procurement is the presence of an imminent threat to life or property and the time element. The City Government needs to address the crisis at hand as quickly as it can. As far as compliance to RA 9184 when it comes to emergency procurement, the LGU has been fully compliant.

There is no prohibition, to the knowledge of the LGU that restricts authorized personnel of the LGU to pay winning suppliers in cash and have it reimbursed by the City Government. However, the LGU only does this in cases where a cash advance by the Disbursing Officer for cash payment will be mainly rendered useless especially if delivery of items is time constrained such that it must be delivered on an actual and specific activity date and that undergoing the same for check payment processes or drawing a cash advance for it will overshoot the said date. Again, time element is emphasized in this situation as the disbursements mentioned in the AOM are mostly transactions related to COVID-19 response.

As to withholding of taxes on reimbursements, CTA decision on case 7989 promulgated on August 8, 2013 reiterates BIR Ruling No. DA-384-98 dated August 24, 1998, regarding the nature of reimbursement of expenses, to wit:

“By its very nature, reimbursement of expenses is not income but merely a return of capital. Accordingly, said reimbursement is not subject to withholding tax”.

We recommended that Management establish sex-disaggregated data for its own personnel and from there conduct gender analysis to identify gender issues and appropriate PPAs to address the gender issues successfully and incorporate them in the GPB for costing.
We recommended that Management establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) System as required under Sections 3.4 and 5.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We reiterated our recommendation that management enforce religiously the submission of copies of contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents within five (5) working days from execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001. 
Otherwise, subject responsible officials and employees to administrative disciplinary action provided in (a) Section 127 PD 1445; (b) Section 55, Title 1-B, Book V of the Revised Administration Code of 1987; and (c) Section 11 of Republic Act No. 6713. Likewise, transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents shall be suspended/disallowed in audit, whichever is necessary.

We also recommended that agency officials responsible for accepting deliveries of procured items to furnish the auditors copies of delivery documents/inspection and acceptance report within twenty four (24) hours after acceptance pursuant to Section 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002 to enable this office to conduct timely review and validation and inspection of delivered items.

We recommended that the BAC invite in writing a representative from COA as observer in stages of the procurement process in order to strictly comply with the provisions of Section 13.1 and 13.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the management should establish internal control policies and procedures over Property and Supply Management system to	
Safeguard the accuracy and reliability of the information by preventing and detecting errors;
Prevent or detect loss of assets and records on a timely basis; and
Protect against waste in all aspects of operation and discourage other types of inefficient use of resources; and
Provide reasonable assurance that those procedures and rules are adhered to by the agency personnel.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant review all the entries in the subsidiary and general ledgers, if any, of the accounts with abnormal balances to determine the original entries made and the cause of these abnormal balances and to immediately prepare appropriate adjustments to reflect the correct balances. Also, to properly maintain SL for liability accounts to prevent incurrence of negative balances of the accounts.

We recommended that the official drivers of the Local Government and other concerned personnel to prepare and submit the Monthly Report of Official Travel and the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977. The Monthly reports will be the summary of trips per vehicle in chronological order of the serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets.

We recommended that all accountable officers to remit and deposit their collection intact at the end of the day as required by applicable laws, rules and regulations.  Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer or Liquidating Officer should always monitor the activity of her staff most particularly those relating to the collection and liquidation aspects as well as in the receipt and issuance of accountable forms to ensure that loss or wastage of government funds and property arising from theft,  malversation and  misuse are prevented.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant should coordinate each other and regularly reconcile the cashbook balances with that appearing per books of account of the LGU monthly or at least quarterly for timely adjustments discrepancies/errors for accurate and fair financial statement presentation.
Management should take immediate steps to work for the processing of the land title for the property it claims to own. To protect government interest.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to remit the amount of P 1,567,726.21 to authorized collecting agent to avoid payment of penalties and surcharges due to its late remittance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be required to thoroughly review past transactions and the journal entries made on the reciprocal accounts in order that the necessary adjustments be effected. So as not to further expand the variance appearing in the account, the accountant shall closely supervise accounting personnel in charge of the General, Special Education and Trust Funds to ensure completeness and accuracy in the recording of transactions in these accounts.

We recommended that Management:

require the refund of COLA paid to public schools’ personnel and Cash Incentives/Financial Assistance/Honorarium paid to Barangay and SK officials otherwise, Notice of Disallowance shall be issued.
stop paying COLA of teachers charged to the coffers of the city since the same allowance is already integrated to their salary and paid by National Government.
refrain from granting/extending financial subsidy to Bgy. Officials/personnel in the form of payment of personal services expenditures (i.e. payment of salaries including honoraria, allowances, bonus and similar forms of compensation.)
observations.managementAction:
As to the Refund of COLA:
1)The City Mayor and other officials concerned shall comply with 
AOM No. 2020-015 specifically on the recommendation to stop the paying of COLA to teachers and personnel charged to the coffers of the City effective after the receipt of AOM, and require the refund of the said payments to concerned DepEd personnel. 

2) To inform concerned DepEd personnel of non-acceptance of claims for City COLA, and send the corresponding amounts to be refunded by individual DepEd personnel.
 As to the refund of assistance paid to Bgy. Officials, the President of the Liga ng mga Barangay, acceded to effect and comply with this recommendation.
observations.reason:
Action Plan 1 and 2 were 
 already done, and the Accounting Office has already been tasked to determine the corresponding amounts to be refunded by each and every school personnel.

Likewise, the same task above for the barangay officials/personnel.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management 

refrain from charging against the Local Development Fund, programs, projects or activities  not included in the Local Development Plan for the Budget Year.  And, an in-depth study and planning be conducted to ensure feasible implementation of the identified PPAs for the year.
observations.managementAction: The 2011-20 LDIP and 2020 AIP have been amended twice during the two CDC Meetings in January and June 2020, and subsequently approved by the SP, to generate a more responsive investment program that will serve as basis for budgeting and project implementation.
observations.reason: No management reason was given for not implementing this audit findings

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management 

consider investing in high yield/combined time deposits instead of several small placements on different due dates and request from depository banks to extend higher interest rates thereon as one of their institutional/valued clients to maximize its earned revenues.
observations.managementAction: Please see letter of the Treasurer dated June 23, 2020 to Mrs. Edna Belmonte, City Auditor

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

direct the concerned offices to prepare   Subsidiary Ledger per collecting officer to duly support the balances reflected in the General Ledger for General Fund, Special Education Fund and Trust Fund;
instruct the Electronic Data Processing Department to provide access to the City Accounting Office to view the daily collections per collecting officer to facilitate preparation of subsidiary ledgers and validation of reports forwarded by the CTO; and
exert best effort to enhance the existing ETRACS so that all collections of the City are recorded in it and if possible, deposits are also recorded therein and be accessed by the City Accounting Office.
observations.managementAction:
Enhanced ETRACS not yet in place.

Deposits are already recorded in ETRACS but some collections are still done manually.

Management explained that the CTO has been maintaining a policy that all collections for the day shall be remitted to the Cashier at the end of the day, leaving therefore no cash collection remain in custody of the collectors at the end of the day. To support their claim, they submitted a copy of the summary of the Cashier’s daily collections showing how she accounts the deposits for the day’s collection.
observations.reason: Enhanced ETRACS not yet in place.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the responsible officials of NCCW, Metro- PESO and GAD Focal Person, in their capacity 

harmonize the functions and responsibilities on the GAD Barangay GROW Negosyo Program of the METRO- PESO and NCCW to address the glitches in the implementation of the said program and be able to fully cater to the intended beneficiaries and attain desired results.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Exert extra effort to trace the unidentified beginning balances, verify the supporting documents to establish its validity, reconcile the amounts between the controlling account, the General Ledger (GL), and the SL, and effect the necessary adjusting and/or correcting entries, thereof, to have a fair presentation of accounts;
To submit an analysis of the accounts mentioned above showing the existing or cumulative balances per year to determine cut-off; and indicate the person/s responsible for the recording and reporting of transactions during the accounting period to determine accountability and liability thereof; and
Consider assigning personnel/s to focus on this area to ensure settlement/compliance of this long outstanding issue.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office, as in-charge of the keeping of the books of accounts, use the proper accounts under the IPSAS, and faithfully adhere to its implementation to ensure a fair presentation of Trust Fund accounts in the Municipality’s financial statements; submit a correct and accurate financial report considering the receipt and utilization of TF accounts and the corresponding accounts relative to its receipt, utilization and liquidation.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Perform a detailed review and analysis of the affected accounts and prepare the necessary correcting journal entry vouchers to have a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements; and
Strengthen accounting controls by performing a regular review on the journal entry and posting in the ledgers made by the accounting staff.

Recommendation
We recommended that management require:
The Accounting and Budget Office to maintain special accounts for every loan obtained using the proper account codes as prescribed presently in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.
The Accounting unit to properly maintain its subsidiary ledgers under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
The Accountant submit a separate and updated Schedule of Loan Amortization for the loans to serve as basis for the Finance Committee in the preparation of the LGU’s Annual Budget and for the Accounting Office to make the necessary adjustments of the recorded loan amortizations, to show the correct amounts actually paid for the principal and the loan interest.
The Local Finance Committee to ensure that disbursements out of loan proceeds are fully documented and all loan related transactions are recorded and monitored.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management require the accounting unit personnel:
To observe proper recording of transactions, maintain respective records which contains complete data/information, which in turn will support the accounts as appearing in the financial statements; and
To prepare the JEV to adjust and/or correct entries, as necessary.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office, as in-charge of the keeping of the books of accounts, use the proper accounts under the IPSAS, and faithfully adhere to its implementation to ensure a fair presentation of Trust Fund accounts in the Municipality’s financial statements; submit a correct and accurate financial report considering the receipt and utilization of TF accounts and the corresponding accounts relative to its receipt, utilization and liquidation.

Recommendation
We recommended that:
The Finance Committee require the concerned Accountable Officers to render accounts and subsequent turnover of accountabilities in accordance with the policies, rules, regulations and procedures and with all other related accounting and auditing rules and regulations.
The Human Resource Management Division (HRMD)/Administrative Division to ensure that proper turn-over of accountabilities is affected in cases of change/s in assignments of accountable officers. Clearances from LGUs finance and other departments, as well as, clearance issued from COA, are mandatory. Hence, notice duly informing COA is necessary to perform cash examination as a prerequisite in issuance of clearance from accountability.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Finance Management to:
Instruct the Accountable Officers, Collecting Agents and Treasurer to strictly comply with the preparation and submission of the monthly RAAF as basis for monitoring the movement of accountable forms at their possession to ensure reliability of the monthly balances of inventory; and
Prepare the Consolidated RAAF in the proper form and presentation and submit it accompanied by individual reports at the end of each period.

Recommendation
We recommended that the management faithfully adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 in all of its procurement activities so as not to defeat the purpose of the law to have a judicious use of the scarce government resources; and to submit the required supporting documents, explanations and/or compliance of requirements as indicated in respective voucher to facilitate proper disposition of subject transaction documents and that vouchers onwards be duly processed and approved before they will be paid to avoid possible suspension and/or disallowance in audit.
We recommended that the City Social Welfare Development Office (CSWDO) coordinate with the DSWD Regional Office on the revalidation if these beneficiaries are indeed ineligible.

We recommended that CSWDO verify the status of the above beneficiaries and check whether they were disqualified from receiving the second tranche of the cash assistance.
Further, if these beneficiaries were still included in the 2nd tranche, we recommended that the CSWDO facilitate communication for the return of cash assistance received from the LGU

We recommended that Management require the concerned personnel to prepare and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued together with the copies of Requisition and Issue Slips to the Accounting Section so that the necessary accounting journal entries be prepared in order to reflect the correct inventory and expense account balances of the BGCM Fund.
We also recommended that Management adhere strictly to the prescribed accounting rules and regulations on the supplies account as required under Section 121, Volume I of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs.
We recommended that Management submit the distribution list for the relief goods distributed to the intended beneficiaries pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445.

We recommended that Management submit an explanation or justification on the deficiencies noted for the Audit Team’s evaluation. We also recommend that Management engage the services of the Local Health Office, being the frontliners for health, in determining the actual needs and requisitions of medical supplies.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Ensure compliance with the provisions pertaining to the receipts and distributions of the donations in-kind received by the City so that they will be properly accounted for; and
b. Direct the CGSO to prepare and submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances together with the required supporting documents within ten working days after the end of each quarter as prescribed in the circular.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
strictly require the City Accountant (CA) to prepare bank reconciliation statements in the prescribed time for all funds (current and time deposit accounts) of the CGN;
exercise LGU’s authority granted under Sections 74 and 111 of PD No. 1445 until they are able to prepare and submit bank reconciliation statements for all funds to the COA Audit Office concerned; and
require the CTO and CAO to reconcile and properly communicate to each other any discrepancies or changes made in the balances reported.

We recommended that Management issue a policy to refrain from the usual practice of manually paying bonuses, incentives and loan moratorium refunds of the city employees by all disbursing officers. Instead, the existing Automated Payroll System be utilized to facilitate the direct crediting of employees’ bank accounts and avoid the possible risk of loss or misappropriation of public funds.

We recommended that Management:
instruct the City Treasurer to prospectively furnish the City Accountant, at the beginning of the year, a duly certified list of the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year;
require the City Accountant to record the RPT Receivable and SET Receivable at the beginning of the year to avoid understatement of receivable accounts and misrepresentation of the financial statements, as a whole; and
examine the underlying cause of the significant balance of SET Receivables amounting to ₱125.66 million, further increased by the unrecorded receivables in CY 2020 amounting to ₱114.83 million, and resolve for appropriate action or necessary reconciliation.

We recommended that Management:
assign a personnel or team who shall be in charge of the proper identification and documentation of properties recorded in the books of CGN;
require the Inventory Committee to timely perform their duties or functions s in conducting actual physical count of PPEs for a specified period of time, to facilitate proper accounting and reporting and correct classification of fixed properties; and
direct the reconciliation of records between the GSD and the Accounting Office to present accurate property records.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to:
review the entries recorded by the accounting staff on withheld taxes and monitor diligently all withheld and remitted amount due to BIR and Pag-IBIG to avoid the incurrence of penalties, charges and sanctions that may be imposed against the CGN;
continuously coordinate with GSIS to facilitate timely reconciliation of records and that records of members are promptly updated and reconciled; and
ensure the generation of accurate list of withheld amounts as basis for the actual remittance within the timelines to avoid under or over remittance.

We recommended that Management:
require the City Accounting Office to remit the Due to LGUs balance in terms of CTC share and RPT share for CY 2020 to the barangays in full and promptly; and
instruct the City Accounting Office to establish the manner and frequency of remitting to the barangay the CTC and RPT shares by identifying the time/schedule of remittance and pin-point who is responsible for processing/requesting/recording of the transaction.

We recommended that Management:
request the NHA to immediately furnish the CGN of the Loan Restructuring Contracts concerning the Isarog Heights Carolina project and Uswag Balatas Resettlement project together with Amortization Schedules being used by the former;
require the City Accountant to make representation with the NHA to reconcile their records; and
upon reconciliation, direct the City Accountant to immediately prepare and record the pertinent adjusting entries, if necessary.

We recommended that Management:
through the CSWDO, look/initiate effort to address the variance on the actual number of beneficiaries and those that are encoded in the system to complete the validation of the beneficiaries and to account for the amount of ₱15.07 million representing the SAP released to the noted variance on beneficiaries served;
assign personnel who will conduct the validation to cover the entire  volume of beneficiaries and ensure that there is no duplication in the granting of benefits and ensure that all listed beneficiaries are eligible/qualified to receive the grant; and
attach the necessary documentary requirements in the liquidation of the cash advance for the distribution of SAP fund as mentioned in the Circular.
We recommended that Management refund the amount of ₱25,780 and any additional amount to be similarly determined to forestall any issuance of Notices of Disallowance (NDs).

We recommended that Management:
instruct the Naga City Development Council to conduct an in-depth planning of Programs, Projects and Activities (PPAs) and prioritization for projects to be included in the LDP and AIP, presenting in details specific items of PPAs embodied therein and facilitate the implementation in order to optimize the prompt delivery of the basic services to the people;
refrain from charging in the 20% Development Fund for projects not specifically identified in the LDF and AIP; and
submit immediately to the audit team the copy of a separate Sanggunian Panlungsod authority to the City Mayor to execute said projects under LDF in order to forestall the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended that Management require the:
City Planning and Development Council to prepare the respective AIP in reference to the COVID-19 Response Fund in the LDF plan and have the same be approved by the sanggunian for its authorization; and
City Budget Office to review and ensure that plans forwarded to their office are attached with pertinent documents that will support its validity.

We recommended that the City Mayor in his capacity as NCSB Chairman:
require the NCSB Co-Chairman and other officials concerned on the compliance with the indicative timelines provided in the Joint Circular No. 2017-1;and
direct the NCSB Administrator, City Accountant and City Treasurer and other concerned person(s) to comply with the indicative timelines provided in the Joint Circular No. 2017-1 to aid the DepEd SDS in his timely preparation of SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 presented by program, activity and project (PAP), by allotment class and per school.

We recommended that Management direct the NCGADC and Sangguniang Panglunsod Committee on Women, through the Drafting Committee, to formulate the IRR within CY 2021 and thereafter publish and disseminate the same for effective implementation of the Naga City GAD Code.

We recommended that Management:
continue formulating responsive programs and projects that would safeguard the environment and would adequately address solid waste avoidance and volume reduction through source reduction and waste minimization measures; and
ensure the proper implementation of the SWM programs and projects which would benefit the city and its barangays through appropriate and environmentally sound solid waste management facilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer monitors the collections and deposits made to avoid over remittances and the Municipal Accountant to make appropriate adjustments to correct the abnormal balance.

We recommended that the:  

Municipal Mayor along with all the concerned officials and employees strictly follow the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 in planning the programs/projects/activities funded by 20% Development Fund and their implementation. See to it that those development projects identified in the AIP were fully implemented for the benefit of the constituents; and

Executive Committee of the Local Development Council identify and prioritize the development projects that could be implemented during the year by taking into consideration the technical manpower of the Municipality.  Consider changes in the annual budget for projects which cannot be implemented during the year to other viable developmental projects, subject to the approval of the Sangguniang Bayan.
We recommended that management observe strict compliance with the documentary requirements enumerated in Section 5.7.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended the following corrective measures for the aforesaid deficiencies:

•  Strict compliance on the documentary requirements under COA Circular No. 2012-001;

• Strictly comply with Section 22 of the Civil Service Memorandum Circular No. 41, series of 1998 and the Civil Service Resolution No. 000034; and,

• Strict compliance with DBM BC No. 2016-2 in the computation of monetization of leave credits.

1We recommended that Management ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the GAD PPAs and the prudent utilization of the GAD Budget in compliance with the PCW/DILG/DBM/NEDA JMC 2013-01 and 2016-01.

We recommended that:

Municipal Planning and Development Officer ensure that the list of on-going PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year is completed and submitted to the Office of the Auditor at the beginning of the year;

Management should not allow informal settlers in the municipal site/government land to protect the interest of the government from the risk of man-made losses/destruction and avoid expenses during eviction especially when the government needs the land to introduce development/improvement.

The City DRRM Office include the allocation from markets and slaughterhouse operations totaling ₱600,000.00 in the LDRRMFIP to identify the program and activities chargeable against the said allocation.

observations.managementAction:
CY 2019 | To state that the BAC did not require the submission of PCAB Licence from the contractors as part of the eligibility requirements is misleading and does not depict the actual scenario. 

The projects subject to the AOM are procured through alternative method of procurement (AMP) - small value. Scrutiny of eligibility-technical requirements, especially the presence of PCAB license, was made in bidded projects. The BAC had justified in its written response and during the exit conference that to address this audit memorandum, it issued guidelines in the issuance of BAC Certificate of Eligibility in the year 2017 for procurement of infrastructure projects made through AMP. The guidelines were reiterated in June 2018 and to ensure that a similar situation is prevented, the BAC resolved that all infrastructure projects shall be procured through competitive bidding. 

All these initiatives were made to address the said audit memorandum to ensure that the procurement law is better implemented by the offices in the local government of the City of Naga, Cebu.

direct the City Accounting Office and Barangay Accounting Office, in coordination with Bgy. Officials to trace and reconcile the difference in their records particularly those of prior years’ fund transfer and its liquidation to determine where the difference lies.

observations.managementAction:
A matrix is hereby enclosed showing the reconciliation of accounts Due to LGUs and Due from LGUs as of December 31, 2019.

Non-Approval by the GSIS of the previous request for change of AAO from the Assessor’s Office to the City Accounting Office. 

Follow up the request for change of AAO  from Assessor’s Office to Accounting Office, and if submitted form can no longer be found, to submit another set of signatories for the change in AAO. (Pls. See attached copy of GSIS Specimen Signatures for submission)
In fairness to the Accounting Office, there has been a regular and diligent  monitoring by the Office In-charge regarding  deficiencies in premiums and loan deductions, but she has no other recourse but to abide with the GSIS Billing 
(100% billing) whether it is correct not in making remittance, just to cope with the timelines of remittance, otherwise, there shall be the imposition of penalties for late payment. 
 A never ending adjustments is being made when the GSIS   sends the correct  billing, meaning, never  ending adjustments and additional remittances are being made when the GSIS has able to   recognize the ARA sent by the HRMO, and able to send the correct billing.

The GSIS should be faulted on the unreconciled balances.  As a concrete evidence of its omission, is their billing for the year 2020.  It only started this September, 2020 that they sent billing based on the new salary rates which took effect in our payrolls way back in January, 2020.  Actual payrolls were already disbursed using the new salary rates, and the payroll system generated the correct premiums, but the GSIS used to send billings using the old salary rates.   

 As a result of which, the Accounting Office  shall be making adjustments for every employee by getting the differences in premiums for the period from January to August, 2020, and additional remittances for the deficiencies shall be made to zero out balances which expresses unremitted premiums.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the City Mayor, in his capacity as NCDRRMC Chairperson: 

appropriate not less than 5% of the estimated revenue from regular sources as LDRRMF to support disaster risk management activities in the current year;
 direct the NCDRRMO to inquire from concerned offices the estimated revenue from regular sources and unexpended balance of STF, after which, prepare a comprehensive  LDRRMFIP that shall present the 100%   for QRF in lump-sum and the allocation for disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness with details as to projects and activities to be funded.  The LDRRMFIP shall include under a separate caption  the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;  and 
require the City Accountant to revert back to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund any unutilized amount of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in the STF after five years which shall be made available for other social services.
observations.managementAction:
For Budget Year 2020, appropriation for LDRRMF has been allocated the mandated 5% of the Estimated Revenue for the year.
Accounting Office consistently monitor funds which are unutilized after 5 years so that reversion will be made at the end of the year back to the General Fund available for other social services.

According to Mr. Reuel Oliver, Head of the ITO, its completion will be this June, 2020. Nevertheless, this was postponed due to pandemic. observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:

Designate a focal person or a committee to handle: a.1) the gathering of other pertinent documents to add support to the existing proof of ownership over the several parcels of lots with a total area of 188,282 sq. m.; a.2) the determination of the appropriate value of the assets, with the assistance of the City Assessor; and a.3) the registration of the untitled land and/or transfer of title in the name of the CGN with the Registry of Deeds;

Instruct the General Services Officer to maintain updated report of the City’s properties and coordinate with concerned Heads of different departments in order for the former acquire custody of all titles and deeds of real properties of the CGN; and
Require the City Accountant to take up in the books all parcels of lots which were officially received by the City as government property at acquisition cost or appraised value in pursuance to Section 63 of P.D. 1445.
observations.managementAction:
There was an on-going search and appraisal of lots to determine the values of the unrecorded parcel of land to be taken up into the books of account. The Assessor’s office, HSDO, SP, Legal Office, GSO and Accounting Office were tasked to prepare such work.
It must be noted that Management failed to submit an Inventory of Land when required in a letter dated September 19, 2017 pursuant to COA LGS, Central Office  directive. 

Also, a legal controversy arose regarding the land where the City Hall and other government offices are located concerning an alleged private owner of the said land. 

Management booked up the amount of ₱1,100,891,000.00 as partial compliance to CY 2017 issued AOM No. 2017-006-2016, after the exit conference conducted on April 17, 2017.

However there were no attached details to properly identify the parcels of lot booked-up in   the JEV.
observations.reason: There was an on-going search and appraisal of lots to determine the values of the unrecorded parcel of land to be taken up into the books of account.

instruct the City Accountant to reclassify the items in the Construction in Progress – Land Improvements (1-07-10-010) account amounting to ₱92,812,017.23 to the proper account Land (1-07-01-010);
require the GSO to maintain complete details of description of each parcel of land such as, lot number, block number and address  for easy reference; and  
require the GSO to relate the costs totalling ₱11,697,172.96, incurred and paid by the City which were directly attributable to a parcel of land, to the specific and actual number of parcels of land recorded in the Land account.

observations.recommendation:

require the City Engineer to monitor and ensure that any request for contract time extension are duly approved and the reasons given by the contractor for any suspension of work or extension of contract time, must be valid and not among those that been considered already in the determination of the original contract time during the conduct of detailed engineering and preparation of the contract documents pursuant to the provisions of Annex E of the RIRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction:
Monitored and ensured that requests for contract time extension are duly approved and reasons given by the contractor must be valid.

Delayed project implemetation has still been noted per Report of PPAs as of December 31, 2017, despite contract time extensions. 

The validity of contract time extensions are being evaluated based on weather conditions and availability of supply of materials, prior to approval of contract time extensions. If the reasons are not valid, liquidated damages are imposed to the contractor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended strict compliance:

with the provisions/sections on the management of funds of the federation as provided for in the Constitution and By-Laws. Before the start of the year, the Federation President is required to prepare a budget and ensure that all disbursements are in accordance with the budget.
Likewise, require that all collections are acknowledged with official receipts. At the end of the year, the Federation Treasurer is required to prepare a financial report to be audited by the Federation Auditor and submitted to the Federation President for inclusion in his Annual Report.  Said report is submitted to the Local Executive Committee of the Panlungsod na Pederasyon/Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) Federation of Naga City.
observations.managementAction:
The  Accomplishment and Financial reports were submitted on time to the National Youth Commission however the SK Federation was not able to send a copy to other concerned offices like COA and the Local Youth Development Council 

The Budget allocated for the SK Federation is under the Naga City Youth Development Fund which is based on the approved PPMP of the City. It will be presented during the budget hearing, which happens a month before the new fiscal year.

The SK Federation President submitted a comment that they are fully aware that it is an essential part of their obligation but takes accountability of their fault on this matter and will ensure that Accomplishment and Financial Reports for the years 2018-2020 will be submitted to other concerned offices.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that management enforce religiously the submission of copies of contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents within five (5) working days from execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001. 

Otherwise, subject responsible officials and employees to administrative disciplinary action provided in (a) Section 127 PD 1445; (b) Section 55, Title 1-B, Book V of the Revised Administration Code of 1987; and (c) Section 11 of Republic Act No. 6713. Likewise, transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents shall be suspended/disallowed in audit, whichever is necessary.

We also recommended that agency officials responsible for accepting deliveries of procured items to furnish the auditors copies of delivery documents/inspection and acceptance report within twenty four (24) hours after acceptance pursuant to Section 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002 to enable this office to conduct timely review and validation and inspection of delivered items.

We recommended that the BAC invite in writing a representative from COA as observer in stages of the procurement process in order to strictly comply with the provisions of Section 13.1 and 13.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that management:
File the request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers to the  Audit Team Leader () and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) after complying with the requirements to support the write-off provided in  Circular 2016 – 005 dated December 19, 2016; and
Exert all efforts to reconcile the long-outstanding fund transfers between the sources and implementing government entities, prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the reconciling items noted, and demand immediate liquidation of balances.

We recommended that the management should establish internal control policies and procedures over Property and Supply Management system to	Safeguard the accuracy and reliability of the information by preventing and detecting errors;
Prevent or detect loss of assets and records on a timely basis; and
Protect against waste in all aspects of operation and discourage other types of inefficient use of resources; and
Provide reasonable assurance that those procedures and rules are adhered to by the agency personnel.

We recommended that:

Municipal Planning and Development Officer ensure that the list of on-going PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year is completed and submitted to the Office of the Auditor at the beginning of the year;
Management fast-track the implementation of all on-going and not yet started development projects programmed and funded under the 20% DF to optimize utilization thereof and timely brings the desired benefits to the intended beneficiaries; and
Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer regularly monitor the status of project implementation for the immediate enjoyment of projects by intended beneficiaries. Also, to include the status of all uncompleted projects of prior years in the Quarterly Reports on Information and Publicity on Programs, Projects, and Activities.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant review all the entries in the subsidiary and general ledgers, if any, of the accounts with abnormal balances to determine the original entries made and the cause of these abnormal balances and to immediately prepare appropriate adjustments to reflect the correct balances. Also, to properly maintain SL for liability accounts to prevent incurrence of negative balances of the accounts.

We recommended that the official drivers of the Local Government and other concerned personnel to prepare and submit the Monthly Report of Official Travel and the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977. The Monthly reports will be the summary of trips per vehicle in chronological order of the serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets.

We recommended that all accountable officers to remit and deposit their collection intact at the end of the day as required by applicable laws, rules and regulations.  Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer or Liquidating Officer should always monitor the activity of her staff most particularly those relating to the collection and liquidation aspects as well as in the receipt and issuance of accountable forms to ensure that loss or wastage of government funds and property arising from theft,  malversation and  misuse are prevented.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant should coordinate each other and regularly reconcile the cashbook balances with that appearing per books of account of the LGU monthly or at least quarterly for timely adjustments discrepancies/errors for accurate and fair financial statement presentation.

Management should not allow informal settlers in the municipal site/government land to protect the interest of the government from the risk of man-made losses/destruction and avoid expenses during eviction especially when the government needs the land to introduce development/improvement.
Management should take immediate steps to work for the processing of the land title for the property it claims to own. To protect government interest.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to remit the amount of P 1,567,726.21 to authorized collecting agent to avoid payment of penalties and surcharges due to its late remittance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be required to thoroughly review past transactions and the journal entries made on the reciprocal accounts in order that the necessary adjustments be effected. So as not to further expand the variance appearing in the account, the accountant shall closely supervise accounting personnel in charge of the General, Special Education and Trust Funds to ensure completeness and accuracy in the recording of transactions in these accounts.

We recommended that these Property, Plant and Equipment should be insured with the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) as required under Paragraph 2, Section 489 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM), Volume I.

We recommended that Management:

direct the concerned offices to prepare Subsidiary Ledger per collecting officer to duly support the balances reflected in the General Ledger for General Fund, Special Education Fund and Trust Fund;

instruct the Electronic Data Processing Department to provide access to the City Accounting Office to view the daily collections per collecting officer to facilitate preparation of subsidiary ledgers and validation of reports forwarded by the CTO; and

exert best effort to enhance the existing ETRACS so that all collections of the City are recorded in it and if possible, deposits are also recorded therein and be accessed by the City Accounting Office.
observations.managementAction: Management explained that the CTO has been maintaining a policy that all collections for the day shall be remitted to the Cashier at the end of the day, leaving therefore no cash collection remain in custody of the collectors at the end of the day. To support their claim, they submitted a copy of the summary of the Cashier’s daily collections showing how she accounts the deposits for the day’s collection.
observations.reason: Enhanced ETRACS not yet in placed

observations.recommendation:
We recommended strict compliance with the provisions/sections on the management of funds of the federation as provided for in the Constitution and By-Laws. Before the start of the year, the Federation President is required to prepare a budget and ensure that all disbursements are in accordance with the budget. 

Likewise, require that all collections are acknowledged with official receipts. At the end of the year, the Federation Treasurer is required to prepare a financial report to be audited by the Federation Auditor and submitted to the Federation President for inclusion in his Annual Report.  Said report is submitted to the Local Executive Committee of the Panlungsod na Pederasyon/Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) Federation of Naga City.
observations.reason: Incumbent SK Chairperson was informed, but has not yet responded.

observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report.

City Accountant to make the necessary accounting entries be made to adjust the account balances.  Stale checks which have been outstanding for more than a year should be reverted back to cash.

Moreover, the bank should be informed of the items that need adjustment in their books of account.

Thereafter, ensure that a copy of corresponding Accomplishment Report be submitted to the Audit Team within five (5) working days from the end of January of the preceding year.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.managementAction: Submission of LCPC Work and Financial Plan
